splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines or criminal statutes.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. This matters because it determines whether agencies can expand criminal liability through interpretive commentary.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary controls over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions, even in criminal sentencing contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' commentary (Application Note 1) can authoritatively expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether Application Note 1 to the career-offender guideline, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor. This determines whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions can be sentenced as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary is valid and controlling
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of the ambiguous guideline text and is entitled to controlling deference, meaning inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as 'controlled substance offenses.'

positions.label: Commentary is invalid — text is unambiguous
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond the text, so it deserves no deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any single criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any one criterion is disqualifying. This significantly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits categorically prohibit it, while others allow it as one factor among many. This affects thousands of prisoners serving lengthy sentences under now-superseded mandatory minimums.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state criminalizes a substance not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled or whether any state-scheduled substance suffices. This affects career offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State schedule controls — no federal limitation
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include state law offenses regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled, finding no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation.

positions.label: Federal schedule required
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the underlying substance is also controlled under federal law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly consider punishment as a purpose when revoking supervised release and imposing a sentence for a supervised release violation.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether punishment is a permissible purpose for sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release, or whether such sentences may only serve rehabilitative and protective purposes. The Sixth Circuit's precedent in United States v. Lewis permits express punishment, placing it at odds with circuits that treat punishment as impermissible.

positions.label: Punishment permitted
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking supervision and imposing a sentence.

positions.label: Punishment not a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that the statutory text limits the purposes of supervised release revocation sentences and does not authorize punishment for the violation itself.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Hyde Amendment attorney's fees motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the longer civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case (triggering the shorter criminal deadline). This determines whether defendants' appeals are timely.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the underlying criminal proceeding, so the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's property belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor when the debtor converts in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. This directly affects whether trustees can sell appreciated assets to satisfy creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A) and § 541(a), post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and legislative history establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, not the estate, to encourage good-faith conversions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as a decision involving a value of 'high order.'
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order.' Other circuits have reached different conclusions about the scope of the collateral order doctrine for sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' that warrants immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because they can be adequately reviewed after final judgment.
